title: Quantitative easing
summary: The Bank of England has published an educational explainer on quantitative easing (QE), describing it as a tool to meet the 2% inflation target by lowering long-term borrowing costs when Bank Rate is constrained. The UK first used QE in March 2009 during the Global Financial Crisis, eventually purchasing £875 billion in UK government gilts and £20 billion in UK corporate bonds, totaling £895 billion. The last QE expansion was announced in November 2020, and quantitative tightening (QT) began in February 2022 as the Bank began reducing its bond holdings.
key_points: The Bank of England's primary monetary policy tool is Bank Rate, with QE as a secondary tool used when Bank Rate is near the zero lower bound.
UK total QE bond purchases reached £895 billion, comprising £875 billion in UK government bonds (gilts) and £20 billion in UK corporate bonds.
QE works by buying bonds, pushing up their prices, which reduces their yield (effective interest rate); lower gilt yields feed through to lower mortgage and loan rates.
When QE is reversed via QT, bonds mature or are sold, causing the digitally-created money used to purchase them to disappear from the economy.
The BoE states QT's purpose is to ensure QE can be used again in future if needed, rather than to directly affect interest rates or inflation.
Research cited by the BoE indicates QE was most effective during market stress: after the 2009 GFC, the 2016 Brexit vote, and the 2020 Covid pandemic onset.
why_it_matters: This Bank of England educational resource provides authoritative institutional context on how QE functions as a monetary policy tool and its historical implementation in the UK. The page explains the mechanics of bond purchases, yield effects, and transmission to broader financial conditions. Key figures confirmed: £895bn total purchases, QT initiated February 2022, and the stated rationale for portfolio unwinding. Note: This source does not provide current policy signals or forward guidance—it is historical and educational in nature.
macro_context: The source explains QE's role in the monetary policy framework: when inflation is below target, QE lowers long-term rates to stimulate spending and lift inflation; when inflation is above target, the BoE raises rates and conducts QT. The page notes that QE's effectiveness varied across 'rounds,' with the largest economic impact after the first round (2009), post-Brexit (2016), and early Covid (2020) when markets were stressed. QT's stated purpose is to ensure future QE capacity, not to directly affect rates or inflation.
market_structure_context: The source describes the operational mechanics of QE: the BoE creates central bank reserves digitally to purchase bonds from asset managers, which increases bond prices and reduces yields. It explains how lower gilt yields serve as a benchmark rate for other financial products including household mortgages. When QT occurs, bond maturities and sales cause the created money to be withdrawn from the economy. The process affects bond markets, equity valuations, and broader financial conditions through portfolio rebalancing effects.
important_numbers: £875 billion in UK government bonds (gilts) purchased
£20 billion in UK corporate bonds purchased
